stem: The homogeneous system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \) has coefficient matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{bmatrix} \), whose third row is the sum of the first two. Because \( A \) is not full rank, the system has nontrivial solutions. What is the rank of \( A \)?
solution: The third row equals the sum of the first two, so it contributes no new pivot. The remaining independent rows give a rank of [[answer]], and the solution space has dimension \( 3 - [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This would require all three rows to be independent, but the third is the sum of the first two.
A matrix with a nonzero row cannot have rank 0.
This under-counts the number of independent rows.
This over-counts the number of independent rows.
A matrix with 3 rows cannot have rank greater than 3.
This is off by one from the correct rank.

stem: The homogeneous system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \) has coefficient matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] & [[d]] \\ [[e]] & [[f]] & [[g]] & [[h]] \end{bmatrix} \), i.e. 2 equations in 4 unknowns. Because there are more unknowns than equations, the system is guaranteed to have a nontrivial solution. What is \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \)?
solution: A matrix with only 2 rows can have rank at most 2, so \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[answer]] \) here.
distractor_rationales: A matrix with only 2 rows cannot have rank 4; rank can never exceed the number of rows.
A matrix with only 2 rows cannot have rank 3; rank can never exceed the number of rows.
Since the rows are not both zero, the rank cannot be 0.
This overcounts the number of independent rows, which cannot exceed 2 here.
This overcounts the number of independent rows, which cannot exceed 2 here.
This undercounts the number of independent rows actually present.

stem: The homogeneous system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \) has coefficient matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] & [[d]] \\ [[e]] & [[f]] & [[g]] & [[h]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since the first two columns already form an invertible \( 2\times2 \) block, the two rows of \( A \) are linearly independent, so \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = 2 \). By the rank-nullity theorem, what is the dimension of the solution space of \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0} \) (the number of free parameters, with 4 unknowns in total)?
solution: By the rank-nullity theorem, the dimension of the solution space is (number of unknowns) \( - \) rank \( = 4 - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the dimension of the solution space with the rank itself.
This forgets to subtract the rank at all, using the number of unknowns alone.
The solution space cannot have dimension 0 when there are more unknowns than independent equations.
This adds the rank to the number of unknowns instead of subtracting.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.

stem: Consider the homogeneous system \( kx + ([[b]])y = 0 \) and \( ([[c]])x + [[d]]y = 0 \), where \( k \) is a real parameter and \( x=y=0 \) is always a (trivial) solution. For which value of \( k \) does the system also have a nontrivial solution?
solution: A homogeneous system has a nontrivial solution exactly when its coefficient matrix \( \begin{bmatrix} k & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) is singular, i.e. \( [[d]]k - ([[b]])([[c]]) = 0 \), so \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bc \); one must still divide by \( [[d]] \) to solve \( [[d]]k = bc \).
This solves the singularity equation with the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The homogeneous system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \) has coefficient matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{bmatrix} \), whose third row is \( [[p]] \) times the first row plus \( [[q]] \) times the second row. This makes \( A \) rank-deficient, so \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0} \) has a one-parameter family of nontrivial solutions. Normalizing the free variable to \( z=1 \), what is the value of \( x \) in such a solution?
solution: The third row is redundant, so the first two rows alone determine the null space. Setting \( z=1 \) and solving \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y=-([[c]]) \), \( [[d]]x+[[e]]y=-([[f]]) \) gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( c \) itself rather than the solved value of \( x \) after normalizing \( z=1 \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
